title: Prince Buster

infobox.value: Cecil Bustamente Campbell  (1938-05-24)24 May 1938  [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), [British Jamaica](/wiki/British_Jamaica "British Jamaica")

[OD](/wiki/Order_of_Distinction_(Jamaica) "Order of Distinction (Jamaica)") (24 May 1938 – 8 September 2016),[[1]](#cite_note-Guardian-1) known professionally as **Prince Buster**, was a [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") singer-songwriter and producer. The records he released in the 1960s influenced and shaped the course of Jamaican contemporary music and created a legacy of work that would be drawn upon later by [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") artists.[[2]](#cite_note-Rodigan-2)

outgoing_links.text: The Guinness Encyclopedia of Popular Music

| Background information | |

| Origin | Jamaica |
| Died | 8 September 2016(2016-09-08) (aged 78)  [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), U.S. |

Musical artist

**Cecil Bustamente Campbell** [OD](/wiki/Order_of_Distinction_(Jamaica) "Order of Distinction (Jamaica)") (24 May 1938 – 8 September 2016),[[1]](#cite_note-Guardian-1) known professionally as **Prince Buster**, was a [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") singer-songwriter and producer. The records he released in the 1960s influenced and shaped the course of Jamaican contemporary music and created a legacy of work that would be drawn upon later by [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") artists.[[2]](#cite_note-Rodigan-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Cecil Bustamente Campbell was born in Orange Street in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), on 24 May 1938.[[3]](#cite_note-Barrow-3) His middle name was given to him by his family in honour of the Labour activist and first post-Independence Prime Minister [William Alexander Clarke Bustamante](/wiki/Alexander_Bustamante "Alexander Bustamante").[[2]](#cite_note-Rodigan-2) In the early 1940s, Campbell was sent to live with his grandmother in rural Jamaica where his family's commitment to the Christian faith, gave him his earliest musical experiences in the form of church singing as well as private family prayer and hymn meetings.[[3]](#cite_note-Barrow-3) Returning to live at Orange Street while still a young boy, Campbell attended the Central Branch School and St. Anne's School.

While at school, Campbell performed three or four times a week at the Glass Bucket Club, as part of [Frankie Lymon](/wiki/Frankie_Lymon "Frankie Lymon")'s Sing and Dance Troupe; rock 'n' roll-themed shows were popular during the 1950s, with the Glass Bucket Club establishing a reputation as the premier music venue and social club for Jamaican teenagers at that time.[[2]](#cite_note-Rodigan-2)[[4]](#cite_note-Gooden2003-4) Upon leaving school he found himself drawn to the ranks of followers of [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)") [Tom the Great Sebastian](/wiki/Tom_the_Great_Sebastian "Tom the Great Sebastian"). Jamaican sound systems at that time were playing American rhythm 'n' blues and Campbell credits Tom the Great Sebastian with his first introduction to the songs and artists that would later influence his own music: [The Clovers](/wiki/The_Clovers "The Clovers")' "Middle of the Night", [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino")'s "Mardi Gras in New Orleans", the [Griffin Brothers](/wiki/Griffin_Brothers "Griffin Brothers") featuring [Margie Day](/wiki/Margie_Day "Margie Day"), and [Shirley & Lee](/wiki/Shirley_%26_Lee "Shirley & Lee").[[2]](#cite_note-Rodigan-2)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Campbell became more actively involved in the operational side of running a [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)") after he was introduced to [Clement 'Coxsone' Dodd](/wiki/Coxsone_Dodd "Coxsone Dodd"), a musically inclined businessman who operated one of Kingston's most popular sound systems.[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) Campbell found himself fulfilling a variety of roles for Coxsone: providing security,[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) handling ticket receipts, identifying and sourcing music as well as working in the essential role of selector. The knowledge he gained about the financial and logistical aspects of staging a sound system dance was put to good use when Campbell made the decision to start his own sound system called 'Voice of the People'.[[2]](#cite_note-Rodigan-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Barrow-3) Campbell approached his family and a radio shop owner called Mr Wong for financial backing; both parties agreed. Campbell's 'Voice of the People' sound system was soon operational and within a short time had established itself as a rival to the sound systems of Coxsone and [Duke Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid").[[3]](#cite_note-Barrow-3) Campbell applied to the Farm Work Program (guest worker scheme for the US agricultural sector) with the intention of buying music for his sound system but, on the day of departure, was refused entry into the scheme. Knowing that he would not be able to personally source records from the US, Campbell decided to record his own music. He approached Arkland "Drumbago" Parks, a professional drummer at the Baby Grand Club who had arranged and recorded a special (exclusive recording) for the Count Boysie sound system. Drumbago agreed to help and Campbell immediately began rehearsing with the musicians at the Baby Grand Club, including the guitarist [Jah](/wiki/Jah "Jah") Jerry, who played on Campbell's first recording session.[[6]](#cite_note-JahJerry-6)

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s")]

In 1961, Campbell released his first single "Little Honey" / "Luke Lane Shuffle" featuring [Jah](/wiki/Jah "Jah") Jerry, Drumbago and [Rico Rodriquez](/wiki/Rico_Rodriguez_(musician) "Rico Rodriguez (musician)") recording under the name of Buster's Group.[[7]](#cite_note-Katz-7) In that same year, he produced "[Oh Carolina](/wiki/Oh_Carolina "Oh Carolina")" by the [Folkes Brothers](/wiki/Folkes_Brothers "Folkes Brothers"),[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) which was released on his Wild Bells label.[[3]](#cite_note-Barrow-3)[[8]](#cite_note-Music-8) The drumming on the record was provided by members of the [Count Ossie Group](/wiki/Count_Ossie "Count Ossie"),[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) [nyabinghi](/wiki/Nyabinghi_rhythm "Nyabinghi rhythm") drummers from the [Rastafarian](/wiki/Rastafarian "Rastafarian") community, Camp [David](/wiki/David "David"), situated on the [Wareika Hill](/wiki/Wareika_Hill "Wareika Hill") above Kingston. After becoming a hit in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), "Oh Carolina" was licensed to [Melodisc](/wiki/Melodisc_Records "Melodisc Records"), a UK label owned by Emil Shalet. Melodisc released the track on their [subsidiary](/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary") label [Blue Beat](/wiki/Blue_Beat_Records "Blue Beat Records"); the label would go on to become [synonymous](/wiki/Synonymous "Synonymous") with 1960s [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") releases for the UK market.[[3]](#cite_note-Barrow-3)

Campbell recorded prolifically throughout the 1960s; notable early ska releases include: "Madness" (1963), "Wash Wash" (1963, with [Ernest Ranglin](/wiki/Ernest_Ranglin "Ernest Ranglin") on bass), "[One Step Beyond](/wiki/One_Step_Beyond_(song) "One Step Beyond (song)")" (1964) and "[Al Capone](/wiki/Al_Capone_(song) "Al Capone (song)")" (1964).[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) The documentary *This is Ska* (1964), hosted by Tony Verity and filmed at the Sombrero Club, includes Campbell performing his Jamaican hit "Wash Wash". In 1964, Campbell met [World Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/World_Heavyweight_Champion "World Heavyweight Champion") boxer [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali"), who invited him to attend a [Nation of Islam](/wiki/Nation_of_Islam "Nation of Islam") talk at Mosque 29 in Miami.[[9]](#cite_note-9) That year Campbell joined the [Nation of Islam](/wiki/Nation_of_Islam "Nation of Islam") and also started to release material, including a version of [Louis X](/wiki/Louis_Farrakhan "Louis Farrakhan")'s "White Man's Heaven is a Black Man's Hell," on his own imprint label called "[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam")". In 1965, he appeared in *Millie in Jamaica*[[10]](#cite_note-10) (a film short about [Millie Small's](/wiki/Millie_(singer) "Millie (singer)") return to Jamaica after the world-wide success of "[My Boy Lollipop](/wiki/My_Boy_Lollipop "My Boy Lollipop")") which was broadcast on [Rediffusion's](/wiki/Rediffusion_London "Rediffusion London") Friday evening pop show *[Ready, Steady, Go!](/wiki/Ready_Steady_Go! "Ready Steady Go!")*[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) Campbell had a top twenty hit in the UK with the single "Al Capone" (no. 18, February 1967).[[11]](#cite_note-British_Hit_Singles_&_Albums-11)
He toured the UK in spring 1967 appearing at the [Marquee Club](/wiki/Marquee_Club "Marquee Club") in May and later toured America to promote the [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") LP release *The Ten Commandments (From Man To Woman)*.
"Ten Commandments" reached no. 81 on the [Billboard Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"), becoming his only hit single in the United States.[[12]](#cite_note-12) By the late 1960s Campbell was once again at the forefront of a musical change in Jamaica; the new music would be called rocksteady. Campbell tracks like "Shaking Up Orange Street" (1967) were arranged with the slower, more soulful rocksteady template as used by [Alton Ellis](/wiki/Alton_Ellis "Alton Ellis") ("Rock Steady") and many others. The album *Judge Dread Rock Steady* was released in 1967, and the title track "Judge Dread" with its satirical theme and vocal style proved to be popular to the point of parody. In 1968, the compilation album *FABulous* was released, opening with the track "Earthquake" (which revisited the theme of Orange Street) and including earlier hits. The album has regularly been reissued in the UK.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### 1970s and beyond

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s and beyond")]

His career slowed up in the 1970s as the predominant style moved away from [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") towards [roots reggae](/wiki/Roots_reggae "Roots reggae"),[[14]](#cite_note-Guardianobit-14) in part because as a [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") he found it difficult to tailor his style towards a [Rastafari](/wiki/Rastafari "Rastafari") audience.[[14]](#cite_note-Guardianobit-14) However he did make an appearance in the 1972 movie *[The Harder They Come](/wiki/The_Harder_They_Come "The Harder They Come")*, which featured Campbell in a cameo role as a DJ.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

He subsequently moved to Miami to pursue business interests including running a jukebox company.[[14]](#cite_note-Guardianobit-14) From 1973 Campbell effectively retired from the music business, with only a handful of compilation albums issued.[[16]](#cite_note-amgbio-16) Even with the new interest in his music following the 2-Tone-led ska revival in the UK in 1979, he remained out of the limelight.[[16]](#cite_note-amgbio-16) Following an acclaimed appearance at the first Reggae Sunsplash event in July 1984 in London,[[17]](#cite_note-17) he resumed performing with [the Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites") as his backing band towards the end of the 1980s, and resumed recording in 1992.[[16]](#cite_note-amgbio-16)

In 1994, a UK court ruled in favour of John Folkes and [Greensleeves Records](/wiki/Greensleeves_Records "Greensleeves Records") after they brought a lawsuit against Campbell and Melodisc (Campbell by this time had acquired Melodisc) concerning authorship of "Oh Carolina".[[18]](#cite_note-ChangPH.1998-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Inc.1994-19) Campbell had a top 30 hit in the UK with the track "Whine and Grine" (no. 21, April 1998) after the song had been used in an advert for [Levi's](/wiki/Levi_Strauss_%26_Co. "Levi Strauss & Co.").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2001, Campbell was awarded the [Order of Distinction](/wiki/Order_of_Distinction_(Jamaica) "Order of Distinction (Jamaica)") by the Jamaican Government for his contribution to music.[[21]](#cite_note-21) He performed at the 2002 Legends Of Ska festival in Toronto.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Other appearances include: [Sierra Nevada World Music Festival](/wiki/Sierra_Nevada_World_Music_Festival "Sierra Nevada World Music Festival") in 2003;[[23]](#cite_note-23) the 2006 Boss Sounds Reggae Festival in [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) the 40th [Montreux Jazz Festival](/wiki/Montreux_Jazz_Festival "Montreux Jazz Festival") in Switzerland with the Delroy Williams Junction Band,[[25]](#cite_note-25) and the 2007 UK [Rhythm Festival](/wiki/Rhythm_Festival "Rhythm Festival").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Campbell resided in Miami, Florida.[[14]](#cite_note-Guardianobit-14)

The Buster, "Girl Why Don't You", was covered by [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") band [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)") for their 2005 album, *[The Dangermen Sessions Vol. 1](/wiki/The_Dangermen_Sessions_Vol._1 "The Dangermen Sessions Vol. 1")*. It was the third cover of a Prince Buster song to be released by the group, following "Madness" and "[One Step Beyond](/wiki/One_Step_Beyond_(song) "One Step Beyond (song)")". The single failed to attract much air play from radio stations and made little impression on the UK Singles Chart.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legacy")]

The UK ska revival at the end of the 1970s that started with the [2-Tone](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records") label from Coventry introduced Campbell's music to a new generation of listeners. In 1979 the band [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)") released their first single on 2-Tone, a tribute to Campbell called "[The Prince](/wiki/The_Prince_(song) "The Prince (song)")".[[27]](#cite_note-Migrates-27) The B-side was a cover of the Campbell song "Madness"[[28]](#cite_note-covers-28) from which they took their name.[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-5) Their second single, released on the [Stiff](/wiki/Stiff_Records "Stiff Records") label ("The Prince" would be the only single released by Madness on the 2-Tone label), was a cover of Campbell's "[One Step Beyond](/wiki/One_Step_Beyond_(song) "One Step Beyond (song)")", which reached the UK Top 10.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

On their self-titled [debut album](/wiki/The_Specials_(album) "The Specials (album)"), [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") covered "Too Hot" and borrowed elements from Campbell's "Judge Dread" (in the song "Stupid Marriage") and "Al Capone" (in the song "Gangsters").[[27]](#cite_note-Migrates-27) The Specials also included a cover of ["Enjoy Yourself"](/wiki/Enjoy_Yourself_(1948_song) "Enjoy Yourself (1948 song)") on their second album *[More Specials](/wiki/More_Specials "More Specials")*.

[The Beat](/wiki/English_Beat "English Beat") covered "Rough Rider" and "Whine & Grine" on their album [*I Just Can't Stop It*](/wiki/I_Just_Can%27t_Stop_It_(album) "I Just Can't Stop It (album)").[[28]](#cite_note-covers-28) Campbell's song "Hard Man Fe Dead" was covered by the U.S. ska band [the Toasters](/wiki/The_Toasters "The Toasters") on their 1996 album *Hard Band For Dead*.[[28]](#cite_note-covers-28)

In 2002, electronic duo Mint Royale sampled Prince Buster for their single "Sexiest Man in Jamaica" on their album *[Dancehall Places](/wiki/Dancehall_Places "Dancehall Places")*.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In September 2002 the tune was released on David Wood and Neil Claxton's Faith & Hope Records label and debuted at no. 20 on the UK singles chart.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Filmmaker [Nicolas Winding Refn](/wiki/Nicolas_Winding_Refn "Nicolas Winding Refn") used Campbell's "Ten Commandments Of Man" on the soundtrack of his 2019 television series "[Too Old to Die Young](/wiki/Too_Old_to_Die_Young "Too Old to Die Young")".[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The Specials, with activist and vocalist [Saffiyah Khan](/wiki/Saffiyah_Khan "Saffiyah Khan"), recorded a rebuttal to "Ten Commandments Of Man", also called "10 Commandments", on their 2019 album *[Encore](/wiki/Encore_(The_Specials_album) "Encore (The Specials album)")*, criticising the original's outdated representation of women.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols") Steve Jones cited Prince Buster as an inspiration in his [autobiography](/wiki/Autobiography "Autobiography") Lonely Boy.

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Death")]

Campbell died on the morning of 8 September 2016,[[1]](#cite_note-Guardian-1) in a hospital in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), Florida, after suffering heart problems, according to his wife.[[34]](#cite_note-death-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) He had reportedly been in poor health for some time after a series of [strokes](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), including one in 2009 that left him unable to walk.[[34]](#cite_note-death-34)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Selected album discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince_Buster&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Selected album discography")]

* *[I Feel the Spirit](/wiki/I_Feel_the_Spirit "I Feel the Spirit")* (1963), [Blue Beat](/wiki/Blue_Beat_Records "Blue Beat Records")[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *[Fly Flying Ska](/wiki/Fly_Flying_Ska "Fly Flying Ska")* (1964), Blue Beat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *National Ska – Pain in My Belly* (1964), Blue Beat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *[It's Burke's Law](/wiki/It%27s_Burke%27s_Law "It's Burke's Law")* (1965), Blue Beat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Ska-Lip-Soul*(1965),[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)
* *What A Hard Man Fe Dead* (1967), Blue Beat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Judge Dread Rock Steady* (1967), Blue Beat/Prince Buster[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Ten Commandments* (1967), [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor")[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Wreck A Pum Pum* (1968), Jet Star[[16]](#cite_note-amgbio-16)
* *She Was A Rough Rider* (1968), Blue Beat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *The Outlaw* (1969), Bluebeat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Big Five* (1971), Melodisc[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Dance Cleopatra Dance* (1972), Blue Elephant[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *The Message Dub Wise* (1972), Melodisc/Fab[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *[Sister Big Stuff](/wiki/Sister_Big_Stuff "Sister Big Stuff")* (1976), Melodisc[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)

Compilations

* *The Original Golden Oldies Vol. 1* (1967), Prince Buster[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Original Golden Oldies Vol. 2* (1967), Shack Recordings[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *FABulous Greatest Hits* (1968), Fab[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *Tutti Frutti* (1968), Fab[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)
* *The Prophet* (1994), Lagoon[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)
* *King of Ska* (2000), Prince Buster/Jet Star[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)
* *Rock A Shacka Vol. 5 – Dance Cleopatra* (2003), Universal[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)

Live

* *Prince Buster on Tour* (1967), Blue Beat[[37]](#cite_note-discography-37)
* *King of Blue Beat* (2001) (reissue of "Prince Buster Live On Tour"), Wah Wah[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)
* *Prince of Peace* (2003), [Island](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records") – Prince Buster with Determinations[[38]](#cite_note-subcultz-38)
